sections.title: 1. Using "kein" (The Noun Negator)
sections.description: You should think of kein as the negative version of the indefinite article (ein/eine). It is used specifically to negate nouns that either have an indefinite article or no article at all.

rules.rule: With Indefinite Nouns: If you would say "I have a book" (Ich habe ein Buch), the negative is "I have no book" (Ich habe kein Buch).
With Uncountable Nouns: For things you can't count, like time or hunger. "I have no time" becomes Ich habe keine Zeit.
Plural Nouns: Since there is no plural for "ein," we use keine for plural negation. "I have no children" is Ich habe keine Kinder.

examples.context: Someone offers you an apple, but it's actually a pear.

examples.translation: I am buying no bread / I'm not buying bread.

sections.title: 2. Using "nicht" (The General Negator)
sections.description: Nicht is much more versatile. It is used to negate verbs, adjectives, adverbs, pronouns, and nouns that have a definite article (der/die/das) or a possessive pronoun (mein/dein).

rules.rule: Negating Verbs: It usually comes at the end of a simple sentence. "I am not coming" is Ich komme nicht.
Negating Adjectives: "I am not happy" is Ich bin nicht glücklich.
Negating Specific Nouns: If the noun has "the" (der/die/das) or "my" (mein), use nicht. "That is not my child" is Das ist nicht mein Kind.
Negating Locations/Prepositions: "I don't live in Berlin" is Ich wohne nicht in Berlin.

examples.example: Ich komme nicht aus Sri Lanka.
examples.translation: I don't come from Sri Lanka.

sections.title: 3. Other Negation Words (Opposites)
sections.description: German has specific pairs of words to show positive vs. negative states.

opposites.positive: noch (still) -> Ich bin noch jung. (I am still young.)
opposites.negative: nicht mehr (no longer) -> Ich bin nicht mehr jung. (I am no longer young.)

sections.title: 4. Why do we use one over the other?
sections.description: The "Why" is based on what you are trying to "cancel out."

explanation.description: If you are canceling the existence of an object (a noun), you use kein. It acts like a "zero quantity."
explanation.example: Ich habe kein Geld. (I have zero money.)

explanation.description: If you are canceling a fact, an action, or a description, you use nicht.
explanation.example: Das Geld ist nicht hier. (The money is not here—you aren't saying the money doesn't exist, you are saying the "fact" of it being here is false.)

sections.title: 5. Summary of Usage Logic
sections.description: Quick reference guide for choosing the correct negation word:

rules.question: Is there a noun?
rules.yes_indefinite: If yes, and it has "a/an" or no article: Use kein.
rules.yes_definite: If yes, but it has "the" or "my/your": Use nicht.

rules.question: Is it a verb, adjective, or name?
